title: Clinopodium majoranifolium
intro: Clinopodium majoranifolium, also known as Mexican oregano or wild marjoram, is a perennial plant in the mint family (Lamiaceae) that is native to Mexico and Central America. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues and reduce the severity of colds and flu. The bioactive constituents of Clinopodium majoranifolium include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, including borneol and camphor, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Clinopodium majoranifolium is often made into teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being used in topical applications such as salves and ointments.
taxonomy: Clinopodium majoranifolium is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae. This plant belongs to the genus Clinopodium, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as wild basil or calamint. Clinopodium majoranifolium is native to South America and is often used as a medicinal herb. Its classification also places it within the order Lamiales, a group of flowering plants characterized by their bilateral symmetry. Further classification of Clinopodium majoranifolium includes its placement within the subfamily Nepetoideae, a subgroup of the Lamiaceae family.
